steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: When water is boiling at 100°C, its temperature…
options: Continues to rise slowly while it boils
Stays constant at 100°C while it boils
Drops slightly because energy is being used up
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! During a change of state the temperature stays constant. The energy supplied is used to break the bonds between particles (increasing potential energy) rather than raising the kinetic energy — that is why we see a flat section on a heating curve.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Temperature stays constant during a change of state because all the energy goes into breaking intermolecular bonds, not raising kinetic energy.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.3.1 — Changes of state

steps.title: Changes of State
steps.bodyHTML: When a substance changes state (e.g. melting, boiling, condensing, freezing) its temperature remains constant even though energy is being supplied or released. The energy during melting and boiling goes into breaking the forces between particles (increasing potential energy), not into increasing kinetic energy. These changes are reversible: the substance can return to its original state without any change to its mass.

relationships: Melting: solid → liquid (energy absorbed, T constant)
Boiling/evaporating: liquid → gas (energy absorbed, T constant)
Condensing: gas → liquid (energy released, T constant)
Freezing: liquid → solid (energy released, T constant)

commonErrors: Thinking temperature rises during a change of state
Confusing boiling (throughout liquid) with evaporation (surface only)
Thinking changes of state are irreversible

aqa.questionText: A heating curve shows how the temperature of a substance changes as it is heated at a constant rate. Describe what is happening at the flat (horizontal) sections of the curve.

steps.label: State what the flat section means
steps.content: The temperature is not changing even though energy is still being supplied at a constant rate.

steps.label: Explain where the energy goes
steps.content: The energy is being used to break the forces (bonds) between particles, increasing the potential energy of the particles rather than their kinetic energy.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — energy used to break bonds / overcome intermolecular forces

steps.label: Name the process
steps.content: This is a change of state — either melting (solid to liquid) or boiling (liquid to gas), depending on which flat section it is.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correctly identifies melting or boiling as the change of state

aqa.finalAnswer: Temperature is constant; energy is breaking bonds between particles (potential energy increases, not kinetic energy); a change of state is occurring.

aqa.questionText: What happens to the mass of a substance when it changes state from liquid to gas?

options.label: A
options.text: It increases because the particles spread out

options.label: B
options.text: It decreases because some particles escape

options.label: C
options.text: It stays the same — no particles are created or destroyed

options.label: D
options.text: It decreases because the particles get lighter

markSchemeSteps: Changes of state are physical changes — no particles are created or destroyed, so mass is conserved ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Options A and B confuse volume change (particles spread out) with mass change — mass is always conserved.
